title: Why is Eva so controversial
wikitext:
[]: Why is Eva so controversial?             Quote Post by Kiel » Tue Jul 20, 2004 8:09 am EDIT: Topic was "Everyone's crazy but me!!!!! No, You're the idiot!!!!" Once again, my random subject titles make no sense, but the subject does...i hope. Point in order: Why is NGE so controvercial? (I'm well aware of the fact that this question is sooooo overused... so I understand if I'm subject to public flogging). It seems that wherever I go and whoever I talk to, there's always this odd stigma to Evangelion. As popular as it is, people chalk it up to being due to its "controvercial" nature. Like we haven't heard that like a bajillion times :roll: But seriously, is it any more controvercial in its portrayal of religious icons/beliefs, psychological head trips, violence, sexual perversion, adolecent related issues, etc... ...than say other anime? Other movies, books or other mediums? What is it about it that makes it stand out like a sore (yet supah populah) thumb? Is it just the popularity that brings it to the front of controversy? Like the comedy starring Ben Stiller and Cameron Diaz... There's something about Eva! *ganbatte!!!*

[]:             Quote Post by Karma Burn » Tue Jul 20, 2004 8:44 am I think what made/makes Evangelion so controvercial is that it was the first and the way it was packaged. Yes, there are many other anime that use religious symbolism and everything else, but it is the way that those things are presented that make it controvercial. Evangelion was the one the first anime to address these issues. It was expected to be a cookie-cutter giant-robot anime, but by the later episodes, you could tell that it wasn't. The characters were a first. They stood out like sore thumbs from other giant robot pilots because weren't brave or good-hearted or anything like that. They were real people. They had real problems. They addressed them in real ways. in any other anime the main character would have jumped in and piloted the eva the first time his father asked him to. He wouldn't have sat there and complained about how he was being used as a tool, while a monster was destorying his surroundings. In any other anime the main character would have been gung-ho, "Sure, what ever you say dad!" about piloting the mech. But in The Director's vision he wasn't. Would have been, if you were in the main character's situation. It was one of, if not the first, publicly broadcasted anime to show it viewers that everything will not be okay. Another thing that makes eva so controvercial is the way it was presented. Trigun uses religious symbolism to an extreme. And its portrayal of christianity, in Wolfwood, could be considered controvercial. Isn't it perverted when Vash is all over women. There is a scene where crawls because he's tied up so he can get a look at a woman panties. How about when he pops those buttons off that girl's shirt, isn't that perverted? But it isn't because Trigun is packaged in a much more lighthearted way. Evangelion is packaged in a much darker setting. The comedy takes a step back and is only used every once in a while. Because the senario for Evangelion is darker so these things stand out. They aren't used for comedic reasons, therefore, people pay greater attention to them. Usually the darker, more depressing, the plot is the more these things, the religious symbolism, the psychological head trips, the violence, the sexual perversion, just seem to stand out. I suppose, though, that in the end, the only thing that matter is how something is packaged. People don't pay attention to the message. They judge something by how it looks, not by what it says. It's like rap music. No one ever listens to the lyrics, they're attracted to the beats and the music. They may have rap songs memorized word for word, but that doesn't mean they know what they're saying. To quote Andre 3000, "Ya'll don't want to listen to me, ya just wanna dance." Its a pretty good way to sum up how people don't listen to the message, they just look at the package. EDIT: Oh well, I tried...

[]:             Quote Post by Dave » Tue Jul 20, 2004 12:34 pm I always thought it was considered controversial because there was a large group of people who absolutely love Eva and a large group who absolutely hate it.

[]:             Quote Post by EVA-01X » Tue Jul 20, 2004 12:44 pm I just chalk it up to the fact that it has all the religious symbolisam and the cross shaped explosions and stuff...

[]:             Quote Post by Asuka Langley » Tue Jul 20, 2004 1:10 pm Dave wrote: I always thought it was considered controversial because there was a large group of people who absolutely love Eva and a large group who absolutely hate it.

[]:             Quote Post by the_seventh_child » Tue Jul 20, 2004 1:12 pm EVA-01X wrote: I just chalk it up to the fact that it has all the religious symbolisam and the cross shaped explosions and stuff...

[]:             Quote Post by EVA-01X » Tue Jul 20, 2004 2:48 pm And I think this brings up an interesting point just how ARE those explosions cross shaped?

[]:             Quote Post by Esion » Tue Jul 20, 2004 2:59 pm I think a lot of it has to do with the series' ending. The origional ending left a lot of question marks. Whenever I hear about Eva it's usually about how controversial and strange it's ending was. Then a little bit later EoE came out and it still left the audience with a lot of question marks. There were no answers to be had and it left the viewers with a sense of incompletion. So it's very different than other animes in that respect. It had no happy ending.

[]:             Quote Post by Phaze » Tue Jul 20, 2004 3:20 pm Esion wrote: So it's very different than other animes in that respect. It had no happy ending.

[]:             Quote Post by Esion » Tue Jul 20, 2004 4:24 pm Well I suppose there are other anime series with strange endings (excel saga and FLCL come to mind). What I should of said is that It's different than you're traditional story format in a lot of ways. A very open ended ending.

[]:             Quote Post by Quiddity » Tue Jul 20, 2004 5:10 pm Karma Burn wrote: It was expected to be a cookie-cutter giant-robot anime, but by the later episodes, you could tell that it wasn't. The characters were a first. They stood out like sore thumbs from other giant robot pilots because weren't brave or good-hearted or anything like that. They were real people. They had real problems. They addressed them in real ways. in any other anime the main character would have jumped in and piloted the eva the first time his father asked him to. He wouldn't have sat there and complained about how he was being used as a tool, while a monster was destorying his surroundings . In any other anime the main character would have been gung-ho, "Sure, what ever you say dad!" about piloting the mech . But in The Director's vision he wasn't. Would have been, if you were in the main character's situation. It was one of, if not the first, publicly broadcasted anime to show it viewers that everything will not be okay.

[]:             Quote Post by Dave » Tue Jul 20, 2004 5:38 pm [/quote] Eva's main point of controversy was the television ending. If they made a typical, cliche ending, like most of the series was, then its controversy would be a fraction of what it is today. The violence from episode 18 and the sex scene, as tame as it was, from episode 20 are the other main attracters of controversy, things that contributed to not only Eva, but many other shows (like Cowboy Bebop) getting screwed over with censorship. One would think the overwhelming factor was the religious symbolism window dressing, but as anyone who has watched the show knows, Eva doesn't actually have any religious content, its meaningless fluff put there to make it more popular. That has added a lot of discussion to it, but isn't anywhere as close to the previous two in terms of attracting controversy.[/quote] Ugh. This two ideas seem to be popular among people who consider themselves eva experts. Gimme a break. That wasn't a sex scene, it was sexual dialogue. And like the violence from episode 18 was all that new to eva (Leliel anyone?). Besides, it was MEANT to shock people, censors be damned. It served a purpose, it wasn't just there to attract young teens obsessed with violence and sex. Cowboy Bepop got censored because the people airing it felt that was what they should do, not because "eva" fucked all the other shows up. As to your second comment, I am well aware of GAINAX's stance on religious content in Eva. Fuck'em. Whether they put it in there as "meaningless fluff" or in order to enhance the story doesn't matter anymore. EVANGELION HAS RELIGIOUS CONTENT. The pilots fight angels, Adam is in the show (and plays a much larger part than just fluff). Lilith as well, and LILITH IS NOT FLUFF! The Jewish mythology surrounding Lilith is of major importance to Evangelion. Just because Eva adapts this mythology (Lilith created man instead of demons, or if you wanna be fancy: the demons Lilith created was mankind) does not mean it isn't there. The Sephirotic tree of life has meaning in End of Evangelion and through the series. It tells you what is happening; it shows you that this person is trying(Gendou)/being forced(Shinji) into a godlike status, an omniprescence. /end Rant.

[]:             Quote Post by Quiddity » Tue Jul 20, 2004 7:56 pm Regarding the violence/sex: I agree with you, but thats irrelevent, because we were not the people who in Japan made a big fuss about that and forced Eva and other shows into giving them the scripts before they aired. It certainly seems tame to us, especially since other shows have outdone what Eva did many times. In America the violence in Eva has never been an issue, infact Eva isn't really that controversial over here anyway. But at the time people did not like it at all, and there was controversy over Eva because of it. Regarding Cowboy Bebop: Cowboy Bebop got censored because of the censorship atmosphere at the time. An atmosphere created much in part thanks to Eva. Had Eva not aired, I think its safe to say that Bebop wouldn't have had 14 episodes removed from its original broadcast. This was posted at the Eva newgroup back in 1996 regarding Eva's true effect on other anime: Hello, I am Toyouchi at Hitatchi System Lab. I have a couple of friends who work in the Anime industry. They told me about the influence of "Evangelion." The most significant issue is that because "Evangelion" series had excessively atrocious and erotic scenes, and GAINAX presented/deliver incomplete products to the TV station, as the result, TV stations began to review the scripts before animated and also they began to order anime producers to present/deliver the products one week before on air. Not only TV-Tokyo*1 but also most of TV stations in Tokyo began these acts. # TV-Tokyo broadcasted "Neon Genesis Evangelion." In some sense, the relationships of mutual trust between Anime producers and TV stations were destroyed by "Evangelion," and consequently, TV stations set up more strict rules for anime production to make it safer. Due to the before-animated-review by TV-Stations, now that Anime producers have to revise scripts a lot. As the result, some anime productions suffer from the too tight schedule which had been carried out without problems before. The friends of mine in the Anime industry say, "if GAINAX wished to make an atrocious and erotic Anime or an experimental Anime, it could have make OVA. More over, they must recognize the significant influence to the entire Anime industry by the fact that the coarse manner in the production resulted those two episodes (They mean the last two episodes)." Since I am not engaged in anime productions, and I heard the opinion above from only a couple of friends in the industry, I don't quite understand how significance the influence is and how long the influence continues. However, as far as I've heard, GAINAX'es attitude of production lacked a consideration.

[]:             Quote Post by Esion » Tue Jul 20, 2004 8:00 pm If you removed all religious content from Eva you'd have to change the plot somewhat.

[]:             Quote Post by MongolSquad » Tue Jul 20, 2004 8:03 pm Not if you stuck strictly to the other plot line where earth was seede by aliens without as many biblical allusions or references.

[]:             Quote Post by Crazyman42 » Tue Jul 20, 2004 8:04 pm ::spoilers:: Actually you would be able to keep the plot of Eva mostly. I mean the Angels point of being there would be changed and all that crap about Adam would change, but Evangelion is mostly character driven. It's story really comes second.

[]:             Quote Post by Esion » Tue Jul 20, 2004 8:08 pm You'd have to change the part about the dead sea scrolls and the sephiroth system. You'd also have to change the reasoning behind the Angels somewhat as well as the reasoning behind the ending of the series (instrumentality/complementation). You'd have to change the plot. Period. Not huge changes but the plot would be changed. I agree that the story is character driven.

[]:             Quote Post by Dave » Tue Jul 20, 2004 8:10 pm The quote you provided regarding Evangelions affect on censoring of certain Anime isn't exactly very.. compelling. The person speaking even admits it is just a rumor he has heard. I still don't think Evangelion had nearly as big an impact on censoring as you seem to believe Quiddity. Regarding religion: I don't believe GAINIAX for even one second. They just don't want to get hassled be religious groups/fanatics everywhere, and I don't blame them. And if they are telling the truth, than they managed to sneak a hell of a lot of religious undertones in without even themselves noticing it. The names are irrelevant eh? Gendou: "This is Bob, the first man!" Sorry but I'm not buying it. I don't understand why you wont just admit EVANGELION HAS RELIGIOUS CONTENT. Sorry, lost the happy. Oh yeah, almost forgot. Know of anyone besides Shinji who had bruises appear upon their palms?

[]:             Quote Post by Quiddity » Tue Jul 20, 2004 8:12 pm Esion wrote: If you removed all religious content from Eva you'd have to change the plot somewhat.

[]:             Quote Post by Esion » Tue Jul 20, 2004 8:14 pm How do they discover third impact? How do they discover instrumentality? What would be the point of instrumentality? How would you end the series?

Once again, my random subject titles make no sense, but the subject does...i hope. Point in order: Why is NGE so controvercial? (I'm well aware of the fact that this question is sooooo overused... so I understand if I'm subject to public flogging)
